This new section, curated by Lagos-based artist Wura-Natasha Ogunji, presents ‘small acts’ – three live performances showcasing new works by Nigerian artists Ngozi Schommers, Ecap Eps, and Taiwo Aiyedogbon. N.H : According to some historians, the origin of performance art resides in the practice of rituals, observed in different ways across cultures. Would you say Performance Art Pavilion at ART X Lagos reflects this historical connection to rituals, and how does the concept of "small acts" contribute to this connection? Wura-Natasha Ogunji, as the curator of the Performance Art Pavilion, likely intended to create a space where the audience could engage with the artists and their performances on a deeper, more personal level. The concept of "small acts" emphasizes the idea that even the smallest actions and expressions can have a significant impact and convey powerful messages. By choosing to focus on "small acts," Ogunji may have drawn a parallel to the rituals that have historically influenced the development of performance art. Rituals, in many ways, are also about the significance of small, repeated actions that hold meaning and purpose within a cultural or communal context. Thus, the "small acts" in the Performance Art Pavilion can be seen as a modern interpretation of rituals, connecting the audience to the artists and their cultural backgrounds in a meaningful and manner. Moreover, the "small acts" concept allows the audience to witness the artists' personal and emotional journeys, as the performances are introspective and intimately connected to their identities and experiences. texts.full_text: Kentucky had over in hemp production: Colorado and Tennessee. The was conducted under the auspices of the Kentucky Department of Agriculture. Research at the University of Kentucky's Spindletop Research Farm sought to improved agronomy and includes research on optimizing cannabinoid yield. The first research crops at Spindletop and Murray State University were planted in May 2014, with seed obtained from California and, after a legal battle with the DEA, imported from Italy. The researchers are also engineering new mechanical harvesters that can reach the high flowers of tall-growing hemp. The first 500-acre commercial crop was planted in Harrison County in 2017, and research permits were issued for over that year. Legal status Under federal law, the THC present in both cannabis and hemp remains a Schedule I controlled substance. Under state law, all hemp grown in compliance with the 2014 farm bill must have a delta-9 THC content not more than 0.3%. Farmers participating in the program must use seeds provided by an educational institution with a DEA license and use varieties expected to be low in THC. A sample of each farmer's hemp crop is tested by the state. Under the 2018 United States farm bill, commodity hemp production was federally legalized. texts.full_text:
use budgeting software such as Mint and Everydollar. As of late, I've lost trust in these "free" services, because I'm not sure how they are "using my data".. I never fully read the terms and conditions anyways. Taking matters in my own hand, I've written offline, open-source budgeting software with Electron. The product is really coming along now, and has made a lot of improvements due to many suggestions from users.

The main features are keeping track of expenses by category/subcategory. There is limited support to sync up transactions from your bank; this was a big feature a lot of people asked forhere's how you can continue the text:

The software also offers detailed reports on where my money goes and allows me to set budgets per category.

texts.full_text: lock on a bank vault, have long been used to secure property. Access is allowed to all who possess a matching “key” device or have “knowledge” of the correct combination. Electronic key cards (swipe cards, chip cards and the like) are little more than modem keys and suffer from the same fundamental limitations of all key based security systems: keys can be lost, stolen, and duplicated. Further, keys do not identify the individual possessing and/or using the key. Modern knowledge based security systems require the correct entry of specific information, into a data entry device, in order to control access. these systems generally require knowledge of an access code that has been selected by a user or has been preset by the system during the user enrollment process. Password protection is commonly employed for computer network security, telephone banking, calling cards, telephone answering services, buildings, and safes. A major limitation of these systems is that they rely on the secrecy of the access code, which can be compromised if the code is shared or discovered by an unauthorized individual, thereby defeating the purpose of the system. Moreover, passwords can be forgotten, requiring additional measures for recovery, and they do not provide a means to verify the identity of the person entering The password, only that the correct sequence of characters has been entered. The processor used in current image processing/inspection systems is not powerful enough to handle the image processing demands, data rates, and algorithms for much of the current generation of (e.g., manufacturing inspection systems), let alone the next generation of systems. Next-generation manufacturing systems have a need for a fast image processing system in order to complete image inspection within required times. As the size of the inspection area and the amount of gray scale data double, the data per one scan area increases dramatically. Therefore, the image inspecti...on process becomes exponentially more demanding on computational resources. To address these challenges, advancements are being made towards specialized hardware solutions that can handle high-speed imaging requirements efficiently. These include FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array) and GPU-based systems which offer parallel processing capabilities well-suited for image data-intensive tasks like those found in advanced manufacturing inspection processes. Moreover, the integration of AI techniques such as machine learning algorithms is increasingly being employed to optimize detection accuracy while reducing computational load by focusing on critical areas or patterns within images. Additionally, cloud computing solutions can provide scalable resources that allow manufacturers to leverage powerful processing power without needing significant investment into local hardware infrastructure. texts.full_text: Capitol Intern. Airways, Inc.v.Capital International Airlines v. United States In the case of Capital International Air Lines, Inc., et al., plaintiffs-appellants versus The United States and others as defendants-respondents (No. 82–5062), a significant legal decision was rendered by the Court of Appeals for the District of Columbia Circuit in its opinion dated June 30, 1983. Background: The case involved Capital International Air Lines Inc., which had been operating under an air carrier certificate issued pursuant to federal aviation regulations and international agreements. The dispute arose when the Civil Aeronautics Board (CAB) decided not to renew this airline's authority for certain routes between New York, London-Heathrow Airport in England, Rome-Fiumicino airport in Italy, Athens-Ellinikon International Airport in Greece, Tel Aviv-Ben Gurion Airport in Israel, and Cairo-Helwan Airport in Egypt. Legal Issues: The primary legal issue was whether the CAB's decision not to renew Capital’s authority for these routes violated statutory or regulatory requirements. The plaintiffs argued that this action by the Board lacked substantial evidence as required under administrative law principles set forth primarily within 5 U.S.C.A §706(2)(E). Court Decision: The Court of Appeals affirmed in part and reversed in part, holding: 1. Substantial Evidence: It found insufficient record support for some aspects of CAB's decision regarding the routes to Rome-Fiumicino Airport. 2. Procedural Requirements: The court determined that certain procedural requirements were not met by the Board when it decided against renewing Capital’s authority. 3. Remand Order: Consequently, parts of the case involving specific route authorities (particularly those concerning Italy) were remanded to CAB for further proceedings consistent with proper legal standards and procedures as outlined in administrative law principles. 4. Other Routes Affirmed: The court upheld other aspects related to different routes where it found that substantial evidence supported the Board's decision. Implications: This case underscored important procedural safeguards within regulatory frameworks, emphasizing adherence by agencies like CAB to statutory mandates when making decisions affecting commercial operations in regulated industries such as aviation. It also highlighted judicial oversight roles ensuring administrative actions are backed with adequate evidentiary support and compliance with due process requirements.

ARTICLE 3A. DEPARTMENT OF VETERANS’ ASSISTANCE §5F-2-1. Department of Veterans' Assistance created within executive branch; purpose, duties generally stated — The Division formerly known as the Division of Veterans Affairs is hereby redesignated and reorganized into a department, to be referred to hereafter in this code or elsewhere by law simply “Department”. The purpose for which said agency was established remains unchanged: To serve veterans' needs effectively through comprehensive assistance programs, advocacy efforts on behalf of West Virginia's veteran population at state government levels as well as federally; and oversight of the Veterans' Council. §6-7-2a. Review by legislative committees — The Joint Standing Committee on Government Organization shall review this Department within two years after its effective date for efficiency, effectiveness, compliance with statutory mandates including those pertaining to veterans' councils’ operations under §9A articles; and make recommendations regarding any necessary adjustments. Pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and interstitial lung disease (ILD) are the most common pulmonary complications in SSc, and PAH is the leading cause of mortality in SSc patients \[[@B2]\]. SSc is a clinically heterogeneous disease with unknown etiopathogenesis and presents with distinct subphenotypes \[[@B3]\]. The classification of these subphenotypes allows us to identify specific molecular pathways that can be targeted for therapeutic interventions. A recent study classified the pulmonary phenotypes in SSc into three clusters: Cluster 1 was characterized by severe PAH, Cluster 2 had a high prevalence of ILD and mild PAH, while Cluster 3 showed no signs of PAH or ILD \[[@B4]\]. These findings suggest that there is heterogeneity within the SSc population regarding disease progression, treatment response, and prognosis. texts.full_text: of epilepsy,[6] a condition inherited from his father. The novelist's grief is apparent throughout the book; Dostoyevsky named the hero Alyosha, as well as imbuing him with qualities which he sought and most admired. His loss is also reflected in the story of Captain Snegiryov and his young son Ilyusha.The death of his son brought Dostoevsky to the Optina Monastery later that year. There, he found inspiration for several aspects of The Brothers Karamazov, though at the time he intended to write a novel about childhood instead. Parts of the biographical section of Zosima's life are based on "The Life of the Elder Leonid", a text he found at Optina and copied "almost word for word".[7]StructureAlthough written in the 19th century, The Brothers Karamazov displays a number of modern elements. Dostoyevsky composed the book with a variety of literary techniques. Though privy to many of the thoughts and feelings of the protagonists, the narrator is a self-proclaimed writer; he discusses his own mannerisms and personal perceptions so often in the novel that he becomes a character. Through his descriptions, the narrator's voice merges imperceptibly into the tone of the people he is describing, often extending into the characters' most personal thoughts. There is no voice of authority in the story (see Mikhail Bakhtin's Problems of Dostoyevsky's Poetics for more on the relationship between Dostoyevsky and his characters). texts.full_text: ny MacIver – January 2013 Corporate users are highlighting the business value they’re deriving from tools – and the shortcomings of email, says David Sacks. The potential of corporate social networking is all too evident at some of the big-name customers of market leader Yammer. The likes of Shell, DHL, Molson Coors, 7-Eleven, Supervalu, Vodacom, LexisNexis, LG Electronics and Pitney Bowes are all keen to talk up the benefits of social media and the breadth of deployments across their workforces of a tool originally thought to be only applicable to knowledge workers. In particular, Supervalu, the US retailer whose regional supermarket brands Lucky, Shop ’n Save, Albertsons, Hornbacher’s and others are clustered in regions around the US, has become a poster child for the value created by Yammer. Historically, its store managers in different regions rarely communicated about ongoing issues, even though they efaced similar challenges related to product availability or staffing shortages. By implementing Yammer as an internal social network across their organization of 150,000 employees and stores spread throughout the US., Supervalu facilitated unprecedented collaboration among its store managers in different regions who could now share best practices on a real-time basis with each regardless of geographical boundaries they were previously confined to. This has resulted not only improved operational efficiency but also enhanced customer satisfaction as issues get resolved faster thanks due largely part because relevant information reaches right people

texts.full_text: The Invisible Visible When you look up at the blue sky, where are the stars that you see at night? They’re there but we can’t see them. A firefly flitting across a field is invisible to us during the day, but at night we can easily spot its flashes. Similarly, proteins, viruses, parasites and bacteria inside living cells can’t be seen by the naked eye under normal conditions. The crystal jellyfish has about 300 photo organs on the bottom edge of the jellyfish’s umbrella. Courtesy Steven Haddock But a technique using a fluorescent protein can light up cells’ molecular machinations like a microscopic flashlight. The first fluorescent protein found in nature comes from the crystal jellyfish, Aequorea victoria, where it is responsible for the green light emitted by its photo organs. It’s called green fluorescent protein (GFP). We don’t know why these jellyfish have this lit-up feature. Fluorescent proteins absorb light with short wavelengths, such as blue light, and immediately return it with a different color light that has a longer wavelength, such as green. texts.full_text: author who achieved public and literary acclaim from his home in Amherst. The Emily Dickinson Museum, nestled in the heart of Amherst, offers a poignant glimpse into the lives of two literary giants: Emily Dickinson and her brother Austin Dickinson. Their adjacent homes, preserved with meticulous care, reveal how the intimate spaces of their daily lives became crucibles for creativity. Emily’s house, with its modest white fence and overgrown garden, reflects her reclusive nature and the seclusion that fueled her poetic genius. The small, cluttered rooms—filled with books, her handwritten poems tucked away in drawers, and the faint scent of lavender—invite visitors to imagine the quiet intensity of her world. In contrast, Austin’s home, more expansive and social, mirrors his role as a progressive thinker and his marriage to Susan Gilbert, a union that sparked both personal and literary tensions. The Dickinsons’ shared history, the whispers of forbidden love and familial duty, seep into the very walls, offering a tangible connection to the themes that permeate their work. Visiting these homes is more than a tour—it’s a journey into the alchemy of place and imagination. The museum’s exhibits, from Emily’s original manuscripts to Austin’s radical political essays, underscore how Amherst’s landscapes and social dynamics shaped their voices. texts.full_text: it connects with readers. The art lies within finding that balance where each serves its purpose without overshadowing the other's effectiveness in conveying a story. Telling: When authors opt for telling over showing, they're essentially summarizing events directly through language—presenting information or stating facts about characters and settings to their audience straightforwardly. This approach can be particularly effective when it comes down to delivering crucial plot points quickly without diluting the essence of those moments with extraneous detail. Showing: On the other hand, showing involves using descriptive details that allow readers a peek into your character's thoughts or experiences through sensory language and actions—it paints vivid pictures in their minds. This can be particularly powerful for building atmosphere; crafting scenes so realistically they feel lived-in by characters whose desires, fears, hopes, and sorrows breathe life into the page. A common misstep is leaning too heavily on one method over another: relying solely on telling to explain every detail or event (which may make your story seem flat), versus overly indulging in showing that slows down pacing unnecessarily. Both have their place; it's about knowing when and how much of each you need. Second Thoughts Second Thoughts The winter holidays bring not only the best in college bowl and NFL playoff games, but this annual time also offers an opportunity for reflection. As we look back on another year that's passed by all too quickly, it’s natural to have second thoughts about decisions made or opportunities missed. In my practice as a chiropractor dedicated to fair journalism—and yes these two can coexist—I've had the chance over three decades of writing and treating patients in parallel paths—to ponder some critical issues. One such issue is how we communicate our knowledge within fields that are often misunderstood, like mine—chiropractic care—which has historically faced skepticism from conventional medical circles. The winter season also invites us to think about what messages resonate with the public during times when people tend towards introspection and resolutions for self-improvement or healing. It's a time where we might question whether our voices are being heard accurately, without bias clouding perception as seen in cases like Steven Salzberg’s critique. So this year end/next beginning cycle is not just about reviewing past articles; it calls us to consider how better communication and understanding can bridge gaps between different schools of thought. How do we ensure that the narrative around alternative health practices such as chiropractic care isn't overshadowed by those who might have a vested interest in maintaining status quo narratives? texts.full_text: in this crazy season. Crazy? Let me explain. Only 10 teams have under 10 losses. It's the season of parity in college hockey. Quinnipiac has the best record -- 23-4-5. The Gophers and Niagara are next at 21-6-5 each. And the arguement could be made that Quinnipiac and Niagara both played easy schedules. And the U of M hasn't exactly been on a roll. The Gophers are 4-3-2 in their last nine games, barely above .500 with a second MacNaughton Cup at stake. Maybe Don Lucia should get that sports psychologist that Tubby Smith had talk to his team before the Gophers victory over No. 1 Indiana in basketball? The Gophers have a chance to do something very odd, which is to either lose or tie with every other team in the WCHA. They have a tie or loss against nine of them. And now they play Denver at home and Bemidji State on the road in their last two series. The Gophers have only one sweep in the conference this season, over Alaska Anchorage at home in January. But the Seawolves tied the Gophers in one game up in Alaska early. REST OF FIELD Based on the current PairWise rankings, the other teams in the NCAA tournament today would be: Four teams from the ECAC, three from the Big East and the CCHA, which will disband after this season, and one from Atlantic Hockey. ECAC: Quinnipiac, RPI, Darmouth and Yale CCHA: Miami, Western Michigan, Notre Dame Big East: New Hampshire, Boston College, UMass-Lowell Atlantic Hockey: Niagara WISCONSIN UPSET Because of the state wrestling tournament behind held at the Kohl Center last weekend, Wisconsin played Penn State, a first-year, independent program on Sunday
